title: A Thousand Splendid Suns

passage.title: A Thousand Splendid Suns (excerpt)
passage.author: By Khaled Hosseini
passage.text:
**In this excerpt, Mariam and her husband, Rasheed, visit a busy market in Kabul, the capital of Afghanistan. Mariam is wearing a [word] for the first time.**

1. They took a bus to a place Rasheed called the Shar-e-Nau Park, where children pushed each other on swings and slapped volleyballs over ragged nets tied to tree trunks. They strolled together and watched boys fly kites, Mariam walking beside Rasheed, tripping now and then on the burqa's hem. For lunch, Rasheed took her to eat in a small [word] house near a [word] he called the Haji Yaghoub. The floor was sticky and the air smoky. The walls smelled faintly of raw meat and the music, which Rasheed described to her as *logari*, was loud. The cooks were thin boys who fanned skewers with one hand and swatted gnats with the other. Mariam, who had never been inside a restaurant, found it odd at first to sit in a crowded room with so many strangers, to lift her burqa to put morsels of food into her mouth. A hint of the same anxiety as the day at the [word] stirred in her stomach, but Rasheed's presence was of some comfort, and, after a while, she did not mind so much the music, the smoke, even the people. And the burqa, she learned to her surprise, was also comforting.

2. It was like a one-way window. Inside it, she was an observer, __buffered__ from the [word] eyes of strangers. She no longer worried that people knew, with a single glance, all the shameful secrets of her past.

3. On the streets, Rasheed named various buildings with authority; this is the American Embassy, he said, that the Foreign Ministry. He pointed to cars, said their names and where they were made: Soviet Volgas, American Chevrolets, German Opels.

4. "Which is your favorite?" he asked.

5. Mariam hesitated, pointed to a Volga, and Rasheed laughed.

6. Kabul was far more crowded than the little that Mariam had seen of Herat. There were fewer trees and fewer *[word]* pulled by horses, but more cars, taller buildings, more traffic lights and more paved roads.

7. And everywhere Mariam heard the city's peculiar [word]: "Dear" was *jan* instead of *jo*, "sister" became *hamshira* instead of *hamshireh*, and so on.

8. From a street vendor, Rasheed bought her ice cream. It was the first time she'd eaten ice cream and Mariam had never imagined that such tricks could be played on a [word]. She devoured the entire bowl, the crushed-pistachio topping, the tiny rice noodles at the bottom.

9. She marveled at the bewitching texture, the lapping sweetness of it.

10. They walked on to a place called Kocheh-Morgha, Chicken Street.

11. It was a narrow, crowded [word] in a neighborhood that Rasheed said was one of Kabul's wealthier ones.

12. "Around here is where foreign diplomats live, rich businessmen, members of the royal family—that sort of people. Not like you and me."

13. "I don't see any chickens," Mariam said.

14. "That's the one thing you can't find on Chicken Street." Rasheed laughed.

15. The street was lined with shops and little stalls that sold lambskin hats and rainbow-colored *[word]*. Rasheed stopped to look at an engraved silver dagger in one shop, and, in another, at an old rifle that the shopkeeper assured Rasheed was a relic from the first war against the British. [...]

16. They strolled past carpet shops, handicraft shops, pastry shops, flower shops, and shops that sold suits for men and dresses for women, and, in them, behind lace curtains, Mariam saw young girls sewing buttons and ironing collars. From time to time, Rasheed greeted a shopkeeper he knew, sometimes in Farsi, other times in [word]. As they shook hands and kissed on the cheek, Mariam stood a few feet away. Rasheed did not wave her over, did not introduce her.

17. He asked her to wait outside an embroidery shop. "I know the owner," he said. "I'll just go in for a minute, say my *[word]*."

18. Mariam waited outside on the crowded sidewalk. She watched the cars crawling up Chicken Street, threading through the horde of [word] and pedestrians, honking at children and donkeys who wouldn't move. She watched the bored-looking merchants inside their tiny stalls, smoking, or spitting into brass spittoons, their faces emerging from the shadows now and then to peddle [word] and fur collared *poostin* coats to passersby.

questions.question_content: **What is the best definition of *buffered*, as it used in paragraph 2?**

note: KA serves 1/4 questions to anonymous users. Passage and sample question captured for skeleton generation.
